devanagari: गोचारणं धेनुकासुरवधः कालियाविषदूषिताम्बूपानान् मृतानां । गवां गोपानां च पुनरुज्जीवनम् । श्रीशुक उवाच । ततश्च पौगण्डवयः श्रितौ व्रजे । ब...
english_devnagari: gocāraṇaṃ dhenukāsuravadhaḥ kāliyāviṣadūṣitāmbūpānān mṛtānāṃgavāṃ gopānāṃ ca punarujjīvanamśrīśuka uvācatataśca paugaṇḍavayaḥ śritau vrajebabhūvatustau paśupālasammataugāścārayantau sakhibhiḥ samaṃ padairvṛndāvanaṃ puṇyamatīva cakratuḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
And after attaining the paugaṇḍa stage (boyhood from the age of six to nine), those two brothers (Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa) were approved (by Nanda and others) as suitable for the job of tending the cows[1]. While grazing the cows and their calves in company of their friends, they rendered the region of Vṛndāvana extremely sacred, by their foot-prints.[2]
purport:
Footnote [1]:
VT. quotes Padma-purāṇa. which states that during their childhood, Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma tended calves. On their attainment of the sixth year, from the 8tḥ day of the bright half of Kārttika, they became bona fide cow tenders. Hence that day is now known as Gopāṣṭamī.
śuklāṣṭamī kārttikasya smṛtā gopāṣṭamī budhaiḥ /
taddinād vāsudeva'bhūd gopaḥ purvaṃ tu vatsapaḥ //

Footnote [2]:
Cowherds used to go bare-footed. Hence the imprints of Kṛṣṇa’s feet on the soil of Vṛndāvana—VT.

devanagari: तन्माधवो वेणुमुदीरयन्वृतो गोपैर्गृणद्भिः स्वयशो बलान्वितः । पशून्पुरस्कृत्य पशव्यमाविशद्विहर्तुकामः कुसुमाकरं वनम् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: tanmādhavo veṇumudīrayanvṛto gopairgṛṇadbhiḥ svayaśo balānvitaḥpaśūnpuraskṛtya paśavyamāviśadvihartukāmaḥ kusumākaraṃ vanam
translation: On one occasion, playing on his own flute, accompanied by Balarāma and surrounded by cowherds singing his glory, Madhava (Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of Lakṣmī as well as the spring) tending his herd of kine in front of him, and desirous of indulging in sports, entered a forest blossoming with flowers and affording a good pasture suitable to the cattle.

devanagari: तन्मञ्जुघोषालिमृगद्विजाकुलं महन्मनःप्रख्यपयःसरस्वता । वातेन जुष्टं शतपत्रगन्धिना निरीक्ष्य रन्तुं भगवान्मनो दधे ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: tanmañjughoṣālimṛgadvijākulaṃ mahanmanaḥprakhyapayaḥsarasvatāvātena juṣṭaṃ śatapatragandhinā nirīkṣya rantuṃ bhagavānmano dadhe
translation: Observing that the (Vṛndāvana) forest was resounded with the sweet notes, sound and humming of birds, beasts (or deer) and bees, and was wafted with a breeze cool due to its contact with pools with waters translucent like (the pure) minds of the great, and fragrant with lotuses, the Lord set his heart to play there.

devanagari: स तत्र तत्रारुणपल्लवश्रिया फलप्रसूनोरुभरेण पादयोः । स्पृशच्छिखान्वीक्ष्य वनस्पतीन्मुदा स्मयन्निवाहाग्रजमादिपूरुषः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sa tatra tatrāruṇapallavaśriyā phalaprasūnorubhareṇa pādayoḥspṛśacchikhānvīkṣya vanaspatīnmudā smayannivāhāgrajamādipūruṣaḥ
translation: Perceiving at every place, trees glowing with the beauty of reddish foliage and touching their feet with their heads—top branches—heavy with a burden of fruits and flowers, Kṛṣṇa, the Primeval Person, slightly smiled out;of delight, and addressed his cider brother.

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । अहो अमी देववरामरार्चितं पादाम्बुजं ते सुमनःफलार्हणम् । नमन्त्युपादाय शिखाभिरात्मनस्तमोऽपहत्यै तरुजन्म यत्कृतम् ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvācaaho amī devavarāmarārcitaṃ pādāmbujaṃ te sumanaḥphalārhaṇamnamantyupādāya śikhābhirātmanastamo'pahatyai tarujanma yatkṛtam
translation:
The glorious Lord said:
Oh! How wonderful! Carrying the presents of fruits and flowers on their heads (top branches), these trees bow down their heads (branches) laden heavily with fruits to your lotus-feet which are adored by the immortals, with a view to absolve themselves of the sin or ignorance (tamas)[3] which caused their birth as trees, Oh prominent God.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
VT. argues that it was ignorance but they deliberately accepted birth as a tree in Vṛndāvana forest, as god Brahmā made a similar request in his prayer (Supra 14.34).
VB. regards these three verses 5,6 and 7 as if prayers from the trees, bees, birds and beasts.

devanagari: एतेऽलिनस्तव यशोऽखिललोकतीर्थं । गायन्त आदिपुरुषानुपथं भजन्ते । प्रायो अमी मुनिगणा भवदीयमुख्या । गूढं वनेऽपि न जहत्यनघात्मदैवम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: ete'linastava yaśo'khilalokatīrthaṃgāyanta ādipuruṣānupathaṃ bhajanteprāyo amī munigaṇā bhavadīyamukhyāgūḍhaṃ vane'pi na jahatyanaghātmadaivam
translation: These (humming) bees, singing your glory that purifies all the world, are resorting to you at every step—you the Ancient- most Person. Most probably, these are the hosts of sages who are prominent among devotees, and they do not let you miss—their personal Deity—even though you lie concealed in the (Vṛndāvana) forest, Oh sinless one!

devanagari: नृत्यन्त्यमी शिखिन ईड्य मुदा हरिण्यः । कुर्वन्ति गोप्य इव ते प्रियमीक्षणेन । सूक्तैश्च कोकिलगणा गृहमागताय । धन्या वनौकस इयान्हि सतां नि...
english_devnagari: nṛtyantyamī śikhina īḍya mudā hariṇyaḥkurvanti gopya iva te priyamīkṣaṇenasūktaiśca kokilagaṇā gṛhamāgatāyadhanyā vanaukasa iyānhi satāṃ nisargaḥ
translation: On your arrival at their home, the peacocks are dancing joyously; the female deer, overwhelmed with joy are giving you delight with their oglings like unto cowherd women (of Vraja), and the flocks of cuckoos welcome you with their sweet cooing. Blessed are these forest-dwellers.[4] For such is the nature of the saintly persons.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Padaratnāvalī regards that words śikhi & Kokila also mean ‘sages—one with matted hair’, and the gods Brahmā (ka) Śiva (u) and others.

devanagari: धन्येयमद्य धरणी तृणवीरुधस्त्वत् । पादस्पृशो द्रुमलताः करजाभिमृष्टाः । नद्योऽद्रयः खगमृगाः सदयावलोकैर् । गोप्योऽन्तरेण भुजयोरपि यत्स्पृह...
english_devnagari: dhanyeyamadya dharaṇī tṛṇavīrudhastvatpādaspṛśo drumalatāḥ karajābhimṛṣṭāḥnadyo'drayaḥ khagamṛgāḥ sadayāvalokairgopyo'ntareṇa bhujayorapi yatspṛhā śrīḥ
translation: [5] Blessed is the earth today; and also the blades of grass and plants touching your feet, and the trees and creepers gently pricked by your nails (to pluck leaves or flowers), and the rivers, hills, birds and beasts that have been viewed at with kind looks, and the Gopa girls who are hugged to your bosom (between your arms) for which even the goddess of beauty, (Lakṣmī) longs.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
This verse is No. 19 in Padaratnāvalī’s Text (in which this ch. is No 13) and is spoken by Śrī Śuka.

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । एवं वृन्दावनं श्रीमत्कृष्णः प्रीतमनाः पशून् । रेमे सञ्चारयन्नद्रेः सरिद्रोधःसु सानुगः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvācaevaṃ vṛndāvanaṃ śrīmatkṛṣṇaḥ prītamanāḥ paśūnreme sañcārayannadreḥ saridrodhaḥsu sānugaḥ
translation: Being delighted at least at the sight of Vṛndāvana (the beauty of which is described above), Lord Kṛṣṇa enjoyed himself along with his companions, while grazing the cows in the pastures on the river bank, in the vicinity of the (Govardhana) mount.

devanagari: क्वचिद्गायति गायत्सु मदान्धालिष्वनुव्रतैः । उपगीयमानचरितः पथि सङ्कर्षणान्वितः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: kvacidgāyati gāyatsu madāndhāliṣvanuvrataiḥupagīyamānacaritaḥ pathi saṅkarṣaṇānvitaḥ
translation: Adorned with garlands and accompanied by Balarāma (Saṅkarṣaṇa)[6], he used to burst out into singing in imitation of the sweet humming of bees intoxicated with the honey sucked by them, while his followers were extolling his feats in song.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Saṅkarṣaṇa is used to indicate that Balarāma being non-different from Kṛṣṇa, joined him in singing—VT.

devanagari: अनुजल्पति जल्पन्तं कलवाक्यैः शुकं क्वचित् । क्वचित्सवल्गु कूजन्तमनुकूजति कोकिलम् । क्वचिच्च कालहंसानामनुकूजति कूजितम् । अभिनृत्यति नृत...
english_devnagari: anujalpati jalpantaṃ kalavākyaiḥ śukaṃ kvacitkvacitsavalgu kūjantamanukūjati kokilamkvacicca kālahaṃsānāmanukūjati kūjitamabhinṛtyati nṛtyantaṃ barhiṇaṃ hāsayankvacit
translation:
Sometimes he imitated the rapturous notes of swans, and sometimes he danced mimicking the dancing peacocks, provoking his companions to laughter.
[Vṛndāvana edition adds:[7]]
11(A). Sometimes he imitated the chattering of the parrots in sweet indistinct words. Sometimes he sweetly cooed in imitation of the warbling and cooing of cuckoos.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
P. 565 Footnote. Source not given but VT’s text comments on this verse and not on V. 11 above.

devanagari: मेघगम्भीरया वाचा नामभिर्दूरगान्पशून् । क्वचिदाह्वयति प्रीत्या गोगोपालमनोज्ञया ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: meghagambhīrayā vācā nāmabhirdūragānpaśūnkvacidāhvayati prītyā gogopālamanojñayā
translation: Sometimes, in a voice deep and sonorous like the rumbling of clouds, and extremely fascinating to the cows and cowherds (alike), he would affectionately call out (by names) cows that have strayed away to distant pastures.

devanagari: चकोरक्रौञ्चचक्राह्व भारद्वाजांश्च बर्हिणः । अनुरौति स्म सत्त्वानां भीतवद्व्याघ्रसिंहयोः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: cakorakrauñcacakrāhva bhāradvājāṃśca barhiṇaḥanurauti sma sattvānāṃ bhītavadvyāghrasiṃhayoḥ
translation: He used to imitate the notes and cries of birds like Cakora (a fabulous bird subsisting on lunar rays), Krauñca (a curlew or heron), cakravāka (the ruddy goose), Bhāradvāja (a sky-lark) and also peacocks, and sometimes behaved or shrieked like animals terrified at tigers and lions.

devanagari: क्वचित्क्रीडापरिश्रान्तं गोपोत्सङ्गोपबर्हणम् । स्वयं विश्रमयत्यार्यं पादसंवाहनादिभिः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: kvacitkrīḍāpariśrāntaṃ gopotsaṅgopabarhaṇamsvayaṃ viśramayatyāryaṃ pādasaṃvāhanādibhiḥ
translation: Sometimes by shampooing his feet and rendering other services (like fanning), Kṛṣṇa would remove the fatigue of his elder brother, Balarāma, exhausted with playing, and lying with his head on the cushion-like lap of a cowherd boy.

devanagari: नृत्यतो गायतः क्वापि वल्गतो युध्यतो मिथः । गृहीतहस्तौ गोपालान्हसन्तौ प्रशशंसतुः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: nṛtyato gāyataḥ kvāpi valgato yudhyato mithaḥgṛhītahastau gopālānhasantau praśaśaṃsatuḥ
translation: Sometimes holding each other by hands, they (Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa) laughingly applauded the cowherd boys who were dancing, capering, singing, prattling and wrestling with each other.

devanagari: क्वचित्पल्लवतल्पेषु नियुद्धश्रमकर्शितः । वृक्षमूलाश्रयः शेते गोपोत्सङ्गोपबर्हणः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: kvacitpallavatalpeṣu niyuddhaśramakarśitaḥvṛkṣamūlāśrayaḥ śete gopotsaṅgopabarhaṇaḥ
translation: At times, being exhausted with (physical) exertions in wrestling, he used to lie on different beds[8] of tender leaves (prepared by his companions) at the foot of trees, placing his head on the cushion-like laps of (elderly) cowherd-boys.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
VT. states that by His Māyā power, Kṛṣṇa lay on all the beds leading each group to believe that Kṛṣṇa was honouring the bed prepared by them.
Subodhinī explains that Kṛṣṇa did so for the sages who were occupying the feet of trees and his beds were the heart of yogins where he lay and he showed his preference and respect to cowherd boys by placing his head on their laps.

devanagari: पादसंवाहनं चक्रुः केचित्तस्य महात्मनः । अपरे हतपाप्मानो व्यजनैः समवीजयन् ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: pādasaṃvāhanaṃ cakruḥ kecittasya mahātmanaḥapare hatapāpmāno vyajanaiḥ samavījayan
translation: Some cowherd boys kneaded the feet of the great- souled Lord while other sinless ones fanned him with fans made of tender leaves.

devanagari: अन्ये तदनुरूपाणि मनोज्ञानि महात्मनः । गायन्ति स्म महाराज स्नेहक्लिन्नधियः शनैः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: anye tadanurūpāṇi manojñāni mahātmanaḥgāyanti sma mahārāja snehaklinnadhiyaḥ śanaiḥ
translation: Others whose hearts (lit. intellect) were steeped in love and devotion for him, sang gently and in sweet low tone beautiful songs suitable for the occasion, and pleasing to him (or songs pertaining to his feats), Oh Great King!

devanagari: एवं निगूढात्मगतिः स्वमायया गोपात्मजत्वं चरितैर्विडम्बयन् । रेमे रमालालितपादपल्लवो ग्राम्यैः समं ग्राम्यवदीशचेष्टितः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ nigūḍhātmagatiḥ svamāyayā gopātmajatvaṃ caritairviḍambayanreme ramālālitapādapallavo grāmyaiḥ samaṃ grāmyavadīśaceṣṭitaḥ
translation: In this way, Kṛṣṇa, by his Māyā, had concealed his course (i.e. real divine nature) and played his role as a cowherd boy by his acts (sports and behaviour). The Lord whose foliage-like tender feet were caressed by goddess Lakṣmī played like a rustic boy with rustics, though occasionally he behaved like the Almighty Lord.

devanagari: श्रीदामा नाम गोपालो रामकेशवयोः सखा । सुबलस्तोककृष्णाद्या गोपाः प्रेम्णेदमब्रुवन् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: śrīdāmā nāma gopālo rāmakeśavayoḥ sakhāsubalastokakṛṣṇādyā gopāḥ premṇedamabruvan
translation:
A certain cowherd called Śrīḍāman was a bosom friend of Rāma and Kṛṣṇa and so were other cowherd boys like Subala, Stoka. Kṛṣṇa spoke to them with friendly affection.
(Padaratnāvalī reads: Śrīdāman spoke to Subala, Aśoka, Kṛṣṇa and other cowherds out of friendliness).

devanagari: राम राम महाबाहो कृष्ण दुष्टनिबर्हण । इतोऽविदूरे सुमहद्वनं तालालिसङ्कुलम् ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: rāma rāma mahābāho kṛṣṇa duṣṭanibarhaṇaito'vidūre sumahadvanaṃ tālālisaṅkulam
translation: “Oh Rāma! Balarāma of mighty arms! Oh Kṛṣṇa, the destroyer of the wicked! Not far from this place, there is a great dense forest thick with rows of palmyra trees.[9]
purport:
Footnote [9]:
VT. locates it with ‘Tālasi’ or ‘Tāraphara’. It is to the southwest of Mathura at a distance of 8 miles from Govardhana.

devanagari: फलानि तत्र भूरीणि पतन्ति पतितानि च । सन्ति किन्त्ववरुद्धानि धेनुकेन दुरात्मना ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: phalāni tatra bhūrīṇi patanti patitāni casanti kintvavaruddhāni dhenukena durātmanā
translation: Abundant fruits lie fallen and have been falling there, but they are all monopolised by Dhenuka of wicked soul.

devanagari: सोऽतिवीर्योऽसुरो राम हे कृष्ण खररूपधृक् । आत्मतुल्यबलैरन्यैर्ज्ञातिभिर्बहुभिर्वृतः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: so'tivīryo'suro rāma he kṛṣṇa khararūpadhṛkātmatulyabalairanyairjñātibhirbahubhirvṛtaḥ
translation: He is a demon of mighty prowess[10], Oh Rāma! Oh Kṛṣṇa! Assuming the form of a donkey, he lives surrounded by members of his tribe (donkeys), all of whom are equal in strength to him.
purport:
Footnote [10]:
v.l. atitīvra—of extremely ferocious nature—Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: तस्मात्कृतनराहाराद्भीतैर्नृभिरमित्रहन् । न सेव्यते पशुगणैः पक्षिसङ्घैर्विवर्जितम् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmātkṛtanarāhārādbhītairnṛbhiramitrahanna sevyate paśugaṇaiḥ pakṣisaṅghairvivarjitam
translation: Being afraid of that cannibal who feeds on human beings, the tract of forest is not visited by men and is also a voided by herds of beasts and flocks of birds.

devanagari: विद्यन्तेऽभुक्तपूर्वाणि फलानि सुरभीणि च । एष वै सुरभिर्गन्धो विषूचीनोऽवगृह्यते ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: vidyante'bhuktapūrvāṇi phalāni surabhīṇi caeṣa vai surabhirgandho viṣūcīno'vagṛhyate
translation: Very delicious and sweet smelling fruits untasted by us lie there. And it is the sweet fragrance of those fruits that has filled the air all around, and we perceive that here also,

devanagari: प्रयच्छ तानि नः कृष्ण गन्धलोभितचेतसाम् । वाञ्छास्ति महती राम गम्यतां यदि रोचते ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: prayaccha tāni naḥ kṛṣṇa gandhalobhitacetasāmvāñchāsti mahatī rāma gamyatāṃ yadi rocate
translation: Oh Kṛṣṇa! Be pleased to give those to us whose hearts are enamoured of their odour. A very strong yearning urges us, Oh Rāma. We make a move if you approve of it.

devanagari: एवं सुहृद्वचः श्रुत्वा सुहृत्प्रियचिकीर्षया । प्रहस्य जग्मतुर्गोपैर्वृतौ तालवनं प्रभू ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ suhṛdvacaḥ śrutvā suhṛtpriyacikīrṣayāprahasya jagmaturgopairvṛtau tālavanaṃ prabhū
translation: Hearing the request of his friend and with a desire to accomplish their wishes, those two mighty gods laughed heartily, and proceeded to the Palm forest accompanied by the cowherds.

devanagari: बलः प्रविश्य बाहुभ्यां तालान्सम्परिकम्पयन् । फलानि पातयामास मतङ्गज इवौजसा ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: balaḥ praviśya bāhubhyāṃ tālānsamparikampayanphalāni pātayāmāsa mataṅgaja ivaujasā
translation: Balarāma entered the forest and by violently shaking the palm trees with his arms like a young elephant, he made the fruit drop down by his physical power.

devanagari: फलानां पततां शब्दं निशम्यासुररासभः । अभ्यधावत्क्षितितलं सनगं परिकम्पयन् ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: phalānāṃ patatāṃ śabdaṃ niśamyāsurarāsabhaḥabhyadhāvatkṣititalaṃ sanagaṃ parikampayan
translation: Hearing the sound of the falling of fruits, the Asura in the form of a donkey, rushed to the place making the earth along with mountains tremble and quake.

devanagari: समेत्य तरसा प्रत्यग्द्वाभ्यां पद्भ्यां बलं बली । निहत्योरसि काशब्दं मुञ्चन्पर्यसरत्खलः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: sametya tarasā pratyagdvābhyāṃ padbhyāṃ balaṃ balīnihatyorasi kāśabdaṃ muñcanparyasaratkhalaḥ
translation: Rushing at Balarāma, the powerful demon kicked with his hind legs on the chest of Balarāma, and braying loudly the wicked fellow ran away to some distance, (moved hither and thither around Balarāma).

devanagari: पुनरासाद्य संरब्ध उपक्रोष्टा पराक्स्थितः । चरणावपरौ राजन्बलाय प्राक्षिपद्रुषा ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: punarāsādya saṃrabdha upakroṣṭā parāksthitaḥcaraṇāvaparau rājanbalāya prākṣipadruṣā
translation: Approaching Balarāma again, the donkey stayed with its face away from Balarāma, and in violent wrath, he flung his hind legs (struck with his hind hoofs) Balarāma, Oh King!

devanagari: स तं गृहीत्वा प्रपदोर्भ्रामयित्वैकपाणिना । चिक्षेप तृणराजाग्रे भ्रामणत्यक्तजीवितम् ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: sa taṃ gṛhītvā prapadorbhrāmayitvaikapāṇinācikṣepa tṛṇarājāgre bhrāmaṇatyaktajīvitam
translation: Catching hold of both his legs with one hand. Balarāma violently whirled him and when the demon died due to that terrible whirling, he hurled him to the top of a tall palm tree.

devanagari: तेनाहतो महातालो वेपमानो बृहच्छिराः । पार्श्वस्थं कम्पयन्भग्नः स चान्यं सोऽपि चापरम् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: tenāhato mahātālo vepamāno bṛhacchirāḥpārśvasthaṃ kampayanbhagnaḥ sa cānyaṃ so'pi cāparam
translation: Being struck (with the carcass of that donkey), the big palm tree with heavy (palm-fruit laden) head crashed, and shook heavily the adjoining tree which fell down, despatching its neighbouring tree to the same fate.

devanagari: बलस्य लीलयोत्सृष्ट खरदेहहताहताः । तालाश्चकम्पिरे सर्वे महावातेरिता इव ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: balasya līlayotsṛṣṭa kharadehahatāhatāḥtālāścakampire sarve mahāvāteritā iva
translation: Being struck down by the impact of the dead body of the donkey which was sportively hurled by Balarāma, all the palm trees shook heavily, as if swept down by a violent hurricane.

devanagari: नैतच्चित्रं भगवति ह्यनन्ते जगदीश्वरे । ओतप्रोतमिदं यस्मिंस्तन्तुष्वङ्ग यथा पटः ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: naitaccitraṃ bhagavati hyanante jagadīśvareotaprotamidaṃ yasmiṃstantuṣvaṅga yathā paṭaḥ
translation: This is not miraculous in the case of the glorious Lord Śeṣa (who incarnated as Balarāma) the Ruler of the universe, in whom the universe is woven warp and woof, like a piece of cloth into the threads, Oh dear child!

devanagari: ततः कृष्णं च रामं च ज्ञातयो धेनुकस्य ये । क्रोष्टारोऽभ्यद्रवन्सर्वे संरब्धा हतबान्धवाः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: tataḥ kṛṣṇaṃ ca rāmaṃ ca jñātayo dhenukasya yekroṣṭāro'bhyadravansarve saṃrabdhā hatabāndhavāḥ
translation: The whole tribe of Dhenuka, the donkeys which flung in rage due to the killing of their brother, dashed at Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

devanagari: तांस्तानापततः कृष्णो रामश्च नृपलीलया । गृहीतपश्चाच्चरणान्प्राहिणोत्तृणराजसु ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: tāṃstānāpatataḥ kṛṣṇo rāmaśca nṛpalīlayāgṛhītapaścāccaraṇānprāhiṇottṛṇarājasu
translation: As they attacked, both Rāma and Kṛṣṇa held them by their hind legs, and flung them easily to the tops of the tall palm trees, as if in sport.

devanagari: फलप्रकरसङ्कीर्णं दैत्यदेहैर्गतासुभिः । रराज भूः सतालाग्रैर्घनैरिव नभस्तलम् ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: phalaprakarasaṅkīrṇaṃ daityadehairgatāsubhiḥrarāja bhūḥ satālāgrairghanairiva nabhastalam
translation: Littered over with heaps of palm fruits, the tops of the palm trees, and the dead bodies of the demons, the surface of the earth looked like the sky overcast with clouds (red, white and blue in colours).

devanagari: तयोस्तत्सुमहत्कर्म निशम्य विबुधादयः । मुमुचुः पुष्पवर्षाणि चक्रुर्वाद्यानि तुष्टुवुः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: tayostatsumahatkarma niśamya vibudhādayaḥmumucuḥ puṣpavarṣāṇi cakrurvādyāni tuṣṭuvuḥ
translation:
Hearing of their marvellous feat, gods and others (such as demigods, celestial damsels etc.) let down showers of
flowers on them, played on the divine musical instruments, and praised them highly.

devanagari: अथ तालफलान्यादन्मनुष्या गतसाध्वसाः । तृणं च पशवश्चेरुर्हतधेनुककानने ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: atha tālaphalānyādanmanuṣyā gatasādhvasāḥtṛṇaṃ ca paśavaścerurhatadhenukakānane
translation: Then the forest being thus rid of Dhenuka, people being free from fear, enjoyed (ate) to the full, fruits of those palm trees, and the cattle grazed in the pasture, fearlessly.

devanagari: कृष्णः कमलपत्राक्षः पुण्यश्रवणकीर्तनः । स्तूयमानोऽनुगैर्गोपैः साग्रजो व्रजमाव्रजत् ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛṣṇaḥ kamalapatrākṣaḥ puṇyaśravaṇakīrtanaḥstūyamāno'nugairgopaiḥ sāgrajo vrajamāvrajat
translation: Kṛṣṇa whose eyes were large like lotus-petals, and the hearing and singing of whose glory is hallowing and meritorious, entered Vraja along with his elder brother Balarāma, in company of his cowherd followers who were glorifying and praising him.

devanagari: तं गोरजश्छुरितकुन्तलबद्धबर्ह । वन्यप्रसूनरुचिरेक्षणचारुहासम् । वेणुम्क्वणन्तमनुगैरुपगीतकीर्तिं । गोप्यो दिदृक्षितदृशोऽभ्यगमन्समेताः ॥ ...
english_devnagari: taṃ gorajaśchuritakuntalabaddhabarhavanyaprasūnarucirekṣaṇacāruhāsamveṇumkvaṇantamanugairupagītakīrtiṃgopyo didṛkṣitadṛśo'bhyagamansametāḥ
translation: The cowherd women rushed out in crowds to welcome and see with eager eyes, Lord Kṛṣṇa, looking charming with sylvain flowers and peacock feathers adorning his curly locks of hair, overlaid with dust raised by (the hoofs of) cows, of beautiful eyes and bewitching smiles, and who was playing on his flute while followed by cowherds singing his glory.

devanagari: पीत्वा मुकुन्दमुखसारघमक्षिभृङ्गैस् । तापं जहुर्विरहजं व्रजयोषितोऽह्नि । तत्सत्कृतिं समधिगम्य विवेश गोष्ठं । सव्रीडहासविनयं यदपाङ्गमोक्...
english_devnagari: pītvā mukundamukhasāraghamakṣibhṛṅgaistāpaṃ jahurvirahajaṃ vrajayoṣito'hnitatsatkṛtiṃ samadhigamya viveśa goṣṭhaṃsavrīḍahāsavinayaṃ yadapāṅgamokṣam
translation: Drinking with their black-bee-like eyes the honey (i.e. charm) in the lotus-like countenance of Mukunda (Kṛṣṇa), the damsels of Vṛaja got subsided their fever of separation from him during the day time. Kṛṣṇa, on his part, entered the cowpen after duly receiving their worship, welcome (consisting) of bashful smiles, ogling with side glances and modesty.

devanagari: तयोर्यशोदारोहिण्यौ पुत्रयोः पुत्रवत्सले । यथाकामं यथाकालं व्यधत्तां परमाशिषः ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: tayoryaśodārohiṇyau putrayoḥ putravatsaleyathākāmaṃ yathākālaṃ vyadhattāṃ paramāśiṣaḥ
translation: Yaśodā and Rohiṇī who were very fond and affectionate to their sons, poured their benedictions suitable to the occasion to their heart’s content.

devanagari: गताध्वानश्रमौ तत्र मज्जनोन्मर्दनादिभिः । नीवीं वसित्वा रुचिरां दिव्यस्रग्गन्धमण्डितौ ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: gatādhvānaśramau tatra majjanonmardanādibhiḥnīvīṃ vasitvā rucirāṃ divyasraggandhamaṇḍitau
translation: They whose fatigue of journey (while tending the cows) was removed by bath, massage etc. in the forest, were dressed in beautiful clothes, and were adorned with excellent garlands, perfumes (sandal paste) etc.

devanagari: जनन्युपहृतं प्राश्य स्वाद्वन्नमुपलालितौ । संविश्य वरशय्यायां सुखं सुषुपतुर्व्रजे ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: jananyupahṛtaṃ prāśya svādvannamupalālitausaṃviśya varaśayyāyāṃ sukhaṃ suṣupaturvraje
translation: They partook of the sweet dish served to them by their mother, and after being caressed by them, they went to sleep in excellent beds, and enjoyed sweet, happy sleep at Vraja.

devanagari: एवं स भगवान्कृष्णो वृन्दावनचरः क्वचित् । ययौ राममृते राजन्कालिन्दीं सखिभिर्वृतः ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ sa bhagavānkṛṣṇo vṛndāvanacaraḥ kvacityayau rāmamṛte rājankālindīṃ sakhibhirvṛtaḥ
translation: In this way,. Lord Kṛṣṇa used to move in the Vṛndāvana. On one occasion, unaccompanied by Balarāma, but in company of (lit. surrounded by) his friends, he went to the Yamunā.

devanagari: अथ गावश्च गोपाश्च निदाघातपपीडिताः । दुष्टं जलं पपुस्तस्यास्तृष्णार्ता विषदूषितम् ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: atha gāvaśca gopāśca nidāghātapapīḍitāḥduṣṭaṃ jalaṃ papustasyāstṛṣṇārtā viṣadūṣitam
translation: There, being tormented with the heat of the summer and oppressed with thirst, the cows as well as cowherds drank the river water polluted due to the contamination of poison.

devanagari: विषाम्भस्तदुपस्पृश्य दैवोपहतचेतसः । निपेतुर्व्यसवः सर्वे सलिलान्ते कुरूद्वह ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: viṣāmbhastadupaspṛśya daivopahatacetasaḥnipeturvyasavaḥ sarve salilānte kurūdvaha
translation: Being deprived of their discretion and judgement by the Providence,[11] immediately after sipping the poisonous water, they all fell dead, by the side of the water[12], Oh best of Kurus.
purport:
Footnote [11]:
Those whose knowledge was affected by the power of the sportive activities of the glorious Lend—VT.

Footnote [12]:
Jīva Gosvāmī, in ṣaṭ-sandarbha (in Kṛṣṇa-sandarbha) explains that Gopas being attendants of the Lord did not die by the poisonous water. This is simply to show that they are human beings as the Lord behaved as a human incarnation.
tad bhāgavata iva nara-līlaupāyikatayā prapañcitam iti mantavyam
For details vide De—Vaiṣṇava Faith aṇd Movement, pp. 55-56. Padaratnāvalī regards this a bit allegorical indicating that the poison of saṃsāra does not affect the devotees of Hari (vide Intro, to ch. 16).

devanagari: वीक्ष्य तान्वै तथाभूतान्कृष्णो योगेश्वरेश्वरः । ईक्षयामृतवर्षिण्या स्वनाथान्समजीवयत् ॥ ५० ॥
english_devnagari: vīkṣya tānvai tathābhūtānkṛṣṇo yogeśvareśvaraḥīkṣayāmṛtavarṣiṇyā svanāthānsamajīvayat
translation: Finding them verily reduced to that condition, Kṛṣṇa, the Lord of the masters of Yoga, restored his proteges to life by his glance that (as if) showered nectar.

devanagari: ते सम्प्रतीतस्मृतयः समुत्थाय जलान्तिकात् । आसन्सुविस्मिताः सर्वे वीक्षमाणाः परस्परम् ॥ ५१ ॥
english_devnagari: te sampratītasmṛtayaḥ samutthāya jalāntikātāsansuvismitāḥ sarve vīkṣamāṇāḥ parasparam
translation: Recovering their consciousness immediately, all of them rose up from the brink of the water and gazed at each other with amazement.

devanagari: अन्वमंसत तद्राजन्गोविन्दानुग्रहेक्षितम् । पीत्वा विषं परेतस्य पुनरुत्थानमात्मनः ॥ ५२ ॥
english_devnagari: anvamaṃsata tadrājangovindānugrahekṣitampītvā viṣaṃ paretasya punarutthānamātmanaḥ
translation:
They came to understand that their restoration to life after death by drinking poison was the result of the gracious look of Kṛṣṇa (Govinda), Oh King Parīkṣit.
[back to top]
